annotations.label: e as limit
annotations.note: Mathlib has `(1 + t/n)^n → exp t` for any real t, which specializes to t=1 to give e, but no dedicated lemma states the e-specific limit.

anchor.snippet: The number e is the limit

annotations.label: e as infinite series

anchor.snippet: It is the sum of the infinite series

annotations.note: The series expansion `exp x = ∑ x^n / n!` is proved generally; specializing to x=1 yields the series for e but no dedicated lemma.

annotations.label: e via slope characterization
annotations.note: No declaration characterizes e as the unique base whose exponential has slope 1 at 0.

anchor.snippet: the unique positive number a such that the graph of the function

annotations.label: Natural exponential function

anchor.snippet: the unique function that equals its own derivative

annotations.note: `Real.exp` is defined and shown to satisfy `HasDerivAt exp (exp x) x`, though uniqueness in the analytic sense is only in `PowerSeries.exp_unique_of_derivative_eq_self`.

annotations.label: e via integral

anchor.snippet: The number e can also be characterized in terms of an integral

annotations.note: No declaration characterizes e as the unique upper bound such that ∫₁^e 1/x dx = 1.

annotations.label: Bernoulli's compound interest limit

anchor.snippet: the constant e occurs as the limit

annotations.note: Specializing the limit `(1 + t/n)^n → exp t` at t=1 gives Bernoulli's limit but no dedicated e-version is stated.

annotations.label: Euler's series for e

anchor.snippet: Euler proved that e is the sum of the infinite series

annotations.note: Specializing `exp x = ∑ x^n / n!` to x=1 yields Euler's series.

annotations.label: Compound interest account

anchor.snippet: An account starts with $1.00 and pays 100 percent interest per year

annotations.note: This is an illustrative example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous compounding limit

anchor.snippet: The limit as n grows large is the number that came to be known as e

annotations.note: Same general limit as Bernoulli's, specialized at t=1.

annotations.label: Slot machine Bernoulli trial

anchor.snippet: Suppose that a gambler plays a slot machine that pays out with a probability of one in n

annotations.note: This is an illustrative example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binomial probability of k wins

anchor.snippet: The probability of winning k times out of n trials is

annotations.note: Mathlib has the binomial distribution but no dedicated lemma for this exact probability formula in the slot-machine framing.

annotations.label: Limit of zero-win probability is 1/e

anchor.snippet: The limit of the above expression, as n tends to infinity, is precisely

annotations.note: The general limit specializes to `(1 - 1/n)^n → e⁻¹` at t = -1, though no dedicated lemma is stated.

annotations.label: Exponential growth

anchor.snippet: Exponential growth is a process that increases quantity over time at an ever-increasing rate

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Asymptotics.ExpGrowth` is unrelated; the informal definition of exponential growth has no formal counterpart.

annotations.label: Standard normal distribution

anchor.snippet: The normal distribution with zero mean and unit standard deviation is known as the standard normal distribution

annotations.note: `gaussianReal 0 1` is the standard normal distribution; the general Gaussian measure is defined with mean and variance.

annotations.label: Hat check (derangements) problem

anchor.snippet: n guests are invited to a party and, at the door, the guests all check their hats

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `derangements α` as the set of permutations with no fixed points, encoding the hat-check problem abstractly.

annotations.label: Derangement count is n!/e rounded

anchor.snippet: the number of ways the hats can be placed into the boxes so that none of the hats are in the right box

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `numDerangements n / n! → 1/e` but does not state the rounding-to-nearest-integer formula.

annotations.label: Maximum of x-th root

anchor.snippet: The maximum value of

annotations.note: No lemma states that x^(1/x) is maximized at x = e.

annotations.label: Breaking stick into n equal parts

anchor.snippet: useful in the problem of a stick of length L that is broken into n equal parts

annotations.note: This is an illustrative example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stirling's formula

anchor.snippet: Stirling's formula for the asymptotics of the factorial function

annotations.note: Stirling's formula is proved as `tendsto_stirlingSeq_sqrt_pi` with an `Asymptotics.IsEquivalent` form `factorial_isEquivalent_stirling`.

annotations.label: Derivative of general exponential

anchor.snippet: A general exponential function y = a x has a derivative, given by a limit

annotations.note: Mathlib has the derivative of `a^x` via rpow (giving `a^x * log a`) though it is computed without going through the limit definition.

annotations.label: Derivative of base-a logarithm

anchor.snippet: considering the derivative of the base- a logarithm

annotations.note: The derivative of the natural log is `1/x`; the base-a log derivative `1/(x log a)` follows by change of base but is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Taylor series for exponential

anchor.snippet: The Taylor series for the exponential function can be deduced from the facts

annotations.note: `exp x = ∑ x^n / n!` is proved in `exp_eq_tsum_div`.

annotations.label: Natural logarithm via integral

anchor.snippet: The natural logarithm function can be defined as the integral

annotations.note: `∫_a^b 1/x dx = log(b/a)` is proved, but log is defined via exp in Mathlib rather than as this integral.

annotations.label: e^x is its own antiderivative

anchor.snippet: it is therefore its own antiderivative as well

annotations.note: `HasDerivAt exp (exp x) x` establishes that exp is its own derivative, hence antiderivative.

annotations.label: Solutions to y' = y

anchor.snippet: is the full solution to the differential equation

annotations.note: Uniqueness is shown at the level of formal power series; uniqueness for real ODE solutions y' = y is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Unique real e inequality

anchor.snippet: The number e is the unique real number such that

annotations.note: Mathlib has bounds on `exp 1` but no characterization of e as the unique base for which the inequality holds.

annotations.label: e^x ≥ x+1 inequality

anchor.snippet: e is the unique base of the exponential for which the inequality

annotations.note: `Real.add_one_le_exp` proves `x + 1 ≤ exp x`; uniqueness of e as the base is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Steiner's problem maximum

anchor.snippet: Steiner's problem asks to find the global maximum for the function

annotations.note: No declaration shows x^(1/x) achieves its global maximum at x = e.

annotations.label: Convergence of infinite tetration

anchor.snippet: The infinite tetration

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize infinite tetration or its convergence region.

annotations.label: e is irrational

anchor.snippet: The real number e is irrational

annotations.note: Search found no `Irrational (Real.exp 1)` declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: e is transcendental (Hermite)

anchor.snippet: by the Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem, e is transcendental

annotations.note: Only the analytical part of Lindemann–Weierstrass is in Mathlib; the conclusion that e is transcendental is not stated.

annotations.label: Taylor series of exponential extends to complex

anchor.snippet: The exponential function e x may be written as a Taylor series

annotations.note: The series identity holds for any normed algebra over ℂ, hence for Complex.exp.

annotations.label: Euler's formula

anchor.snippet: allows one to derive Euler's formula

annotations.note: `Complex.exp_mul_I` states `exp (z * I) = cos z + sin z * I`; `exp_ofReal_mul_I` gives the real-variable version.

annotations.label: Euler's identity

anchor.snippet: The special case with x = π is Euler's identity

annotations.note: `Complex.exp_pi_mul_I` states `exp (π * I) = -1`, equivalent to Euler's identity.

annotations.label: de Moivre's formula

anchor.snippet: for any integer n , which is de Moivre's formula

annotations.note: Stated for ℕ as `(cos z + sin z * I)^n = cos (n z) + sin (n z) * I`.

annotations.label: Entropy of a partition

anchor.snippet: the entropy of which is the expected information gained

annotations.note: Mathlib has `negMulLog` (the entropy summand) and binary entropy, but no general Shannon entropy of a partition.

annotations.label: Simple continued fraction for e

anchor.snippet: there is also the simple continued fraction

annotations.note: Mathlib has continued fractions but no continued-fraction expansion of e.

annotations.label: Infinite product for e

anchor.snippet: The following infinite product evaluates to e

annotations.note: No infinite-product representation of e was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Expected value of V is e

anchor.snippet: Let V be the least number n such that the sum of the first n observations exceeds 1

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize this stochastic characterization of e.

annotations.label: Series for computing e

anchor.snippet: One way to compute the digits of e is with the series

annotations.note: The standard series at x=1 is what's typically used; effective digit bounds appear in `Real.exp_one_near_20`.

annotations.label: Recursive binary splitting for e

anchor.snippet: A faster method involves two recursive functions

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize binary-splitting algorithms for computing e.
